Risk Description: Angular applications are vulnerable to XSS attacks when using bypassSecurityTrust methods (bypassSecurityTrustHtml, bypassSecurityTrustScript, bypassSecurityTrustStyle, bypassSecurityTrustUrl, bypassSecurityTrustResourceUrl) improperly, allowing attackers to inject malicious scripts that execute in users' browsers and steal sensitive data or perform unauthorized actions.
Base Mitigation: Avoid using bypassSecurityTrust methods unless absolutely necessary. Implement strict Content Security Policy (CSP) headers, use Angular's built-in sanitization, and conduct regular security audits of template bindings.

Risk Title: DOM-Based XSS Through Unsafe Property Binding
Risk Description: Using property binding with untrusted data (e.g., [innerHTML], [src], [href]) without proper sanitization can lead to DOM-based XSS attacks where malicious code is executed directly in the browser without server interaction.
Base Mitigation: Use Angular's DomSanitizer service to sanitize all untrusted content, prefer text interpolation over innerHTML, and implement input validation at component level.

Context: HTTP client communication with backend APIs
Risk Description: Angular applications making HTTP requests without proper CSRF protection are vulnerable to attacks where malicious sites can trigger unauthorized actions on behalf of authenticated users.
Base Mitigation: Enable Angular's built-in CSRF protection by configuring HttpClientXsrfModule, ensure backend validates CSRF tokens, and use SameSite cookie attributes.

Risk Description: Improper validation of redirect URLs in Angular routing can allow attackers to redirect users to malicious external sites, facilitating phishing attacks and credential theft.
Base Mitigation: Validate all redirect URLs against a whitelist, use Angular's Router with strict route guards, and avoid using user-supplied data directly in navigation commands.

Context: Local storage and session storage usage
Risk Title: Sensitive Data Exposure in Browser Storage
Risk Description: Storing sensitive information (tokens, PII, credentials) in localStorage or sessionStorage exposes data to XSS attacks and makes it accessible to any script running on the same origin.
Base Mitigation: Store sensitive tokens in httpOnly secure cookies, encrypt data before storing in browser storage, implement token rotation, and use short-lived tokens with refresh mechanisms.

Risk Title: Supply Chain Attacks via Compromised Dependencies
Risk Description: Angular applications depend on numerous npm packages that may contain vulnerabilities or malicious code, exposing the application to supply chain attacks and data breaches.
Base Mitigation: Regularly audit dependencies using npm audit and Snyk, implement Software Composition Analysis (SCA), use package-lock.json, verify package integrity, and maintain a dependency update policy.

Risk Description: Improper handling of JWT tokens or authentication credentials in Angular applications can lead to token theft, session hijacking, and unauthorized access to protected resources.
Base Mitigation: Implement secure token storage (httpOnly cookies), use short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens, implement token expiration checks, and use interceptors for automatic token attachment.

Risk Description: Relying solely on Angular route guards for authorization allows attackers to bypass client-side checks by manipulating browser state or directly accessing API endpoints.
Base Mitigation: Implement authorization checks on both client and server sides, use CanActivate and CanLoad guards as UI convenience only, and enforce all access control at the API level.

Risk Description: Deploying Angular applications with source maps enabled in production exposes application logic, business rules, API endpoints, and potentially sensitive information to attackers.
Base Mitigation: Disable source maps in production builds, use Angular CLI production flag (--prod), configure angular.json to exclude source maps, and implement proper build pipeline validation.

Risk Title: Hardcoded Secrets and API Keys Exposure
Risk Description: Embedding API keys, secrets, or sensitive configuration in Angular environment files results in exposure through client-side code, as all Angular code is delivered to the browser.
Base Mitigation: Never store secrets in Angular code, use backend proxy for API calls requiring keys, implement environment-specific configuration management, and use secret management services.

Risk Description: Inadequate validation of user inputs in Angular forms can lead to injection attacks, data corruption, and security vulnerabilities when data is processed by backend systems.
Base Mitigation: Implement comprehensive client-side validation using Angular Reactive Forms with custom validators, sanitize all inputs, and enforce server-side validation as the primary defense.

Risk Description: Flawed logic in HTTP interceptors can lead to authentication bypass, token leakage to unauthorized domains, or improper error handling that exposes sensitive information.
Base Mitigation: Implement strict domain whitelisting in interceptors, validate token attachment logic, handle errors securely without exposing sensitive details, and conduct thorough testing of interceptor chains.

Risk Title: Weak or Missing Content Security Policy
Risk Description: Absence of proper CSP headers or overly permissive policies fails to prevent XSS attacks, inline script execution, and unauthorized resource loading in Angular applications.
Base Mitigation: Implement strict CSP headers with nonce-based or hash-based script execution, avoid 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval', use Angular's CSP-compatible build options, and regularly test CSP effectiveness.

Risk Description: When using Angular Universal for SSR, improper handling of user input or state can lead to server-side injection attacks, memory leaks, or exposure of server-side resources.
Base Mitigation: Sanitize all user inputs on the server side, implement proper state transfer mechanisms, isolate SSR processes, and conduct security reviews of server-side rendering logic.

Risk Description: Using unencrypted WebSocket connections (ws://) or improper authentication in WebSocket implementations exposes real-time data to interception and man-in-the-middle attacks.
Base Mitigation: Always use secure WebSocket connections (wss://), implement authentication and authorization for WebSocket connections, validate all messages, and use message signing or encryption.

Risk Description: Monolithic Angular bundles without proper code splitting result in large initial download sizes, slow Time to Interactive (TTI), and poor user experience, especially on mobile networks.
Base Mitigation: Implement lazy loading for feature modules, use Angular CLI's budget configuration, enable tree-shaking, optimize third-party dependencies, and implement route-based code splitting.

Risk Description: Default change detection strategy checking all components on every event can cause performance degradation in large applications with complex component trees, leading to UI freezes and poor responsiveness.
Base Mitigation: Use OnPush change detection strategy where appropriate, implement trackBy functions in *ngFor loops, detach change detection for static components, and use ChangeDetectorRef judiciously.

Risk Description: Failing to unsubscribe from RxJS observables in Angular components leads to memory leaks, increased memory consumption, and degraded application performance over time.
Base Mitigation: Use async pipe in templates, implement takeUntil pattern with component destruction, use SubSink or similar libraries, and conduct memory profiling to identify leaks.

Risk Title: Complex Template Expressions Causing Performance Issues
Risk Description: Using complex calculations, method calls, or heavy logic in template expressions causes repeated execution during change detection cycles, significantly impacting rendering performance.
Base Mitigation: Move complex logic to component properties or methods called once, use pure pipes for transformations, memoize expensive calculations, and avoid method calls in templates.

Risk Title: Excessive HTTP Requests and API Calls
Risk Description: Unoptimized HTTP request patterns, lack of caching, and redundant API calls increase network overhead, slow down application responsiveness, and strain backend resources.
Base Mitigation: Implement HTTP caching strategies using interceptors, use shareReplay for shared observables, implement request debouncing and throttling, and optimize API payload sizes.

Risk Title: Performance Impact of Heavy Third-Party Libraries
Risk Description: Including large or poorly optimized third-party libraries increases bundle size, parsing time, and execution overhead, negatively impacting application load time and runtime performance.
Base Mitigation: Audit third-party dependencies for size and performance impact, use lightweight alternatives, implement dynamic imports for heavy libraries, and consider custom implementations for simple functionality.

Risk Description: Frequent or inefficient DOM updates, especially in loops or during rapid state changes, cause layout thrashing and reflow issues, degrading rendering performance and user experience.
Base Mitigation: Batch DOM updates, use virtual scrolling for large lists (CDK Virtual Scroll), minimize direct DOM manipulation, use Angular's Renderer2 for safe DOM access, and implement efficient list rendering strategies.

Risk Description: Heavy or poorly optimized Angular animations can cause frame drops, jank, and reduced responsiveness, especially on lower-end devices or when multiple animations run simultaneously.
Base Mitigation: Use CSS transforms and opacity for animations (GPU-accelerated), limit simultaneous animations, use Angular's animation callbacks to sequence effects, and test on target devices.

Risk Description: Zone.js patches all asynchronous operations, triggering change detection frequently and causing performance overhead in applications with high-frequency events or third-party integrations.
Base Mitigation: Run operations outside Angular zone using NgZone.runOutsideAngular for non-UI updates, consider zone-less Angular for performance-critical applications, and optimize event handling.

Risk Title: Slow Build Times Impacting Development Velocity
Risk Description: Large Angular applications with numerous dependencies and complex build configurations experience slow compilation times, reducing developer productivity and CI/CD pipeline efficiency.
Base Mitigation: Enable Angular Ivy compilation, use incremental builds, optimize TypeScript configuration, implement build caching, and consider module federation for micro-frontend architectures.

Risk Title: Scalability Issues with Component State Management
Risk Description: Managing application state through component properties and @Input/@Output becomes unmanageable in large applications, leading to prop drilling, tight coupling, and difficult maintenance.
Base Mitigation: Implement centralized state management using NgRx, Akita, or similar libraries, use facade pattern for state access, implement proper state normalization, and document state architecture.

Risk Description: Poor module organization with everything in a single module or improper module boundaries makes the application difficult to scale, maintain, and deploy independently.
Base Mitigation: Implement feature module architecture, use shared and core modules appropriately, apply lazy loading strategies, consider micro-frontend architecture for very large applications, and establish clear module boundaries.

Risk Description: Tightly coupled components with hardcoded dependencies and poor abstraction limit reusability across the application, leading to code duplication and increased maintenance burden.
Base Mitigation: Design components with single responsibility principle, use content projection and component composition, implement proper component APIs with @Input/@Output, and create a component library.

Risk Title: Service Scalability and Dependency Management Issues
Risk Description: Poorly designed service architecture with circular dependencies, god services, or improper provider scoping makes the application difficult to scale and test.
Base Mitigation: Apply SOLID principles to service design, use proper provider scoping (root, module, component), avoid circular dependencies, implement service facades, and use dependency injection effectively.

Risk Description: As applications grow, routing configurations become complex and difficult to manage, leading to route conflicts, maintenance challenges, and navigation issues.
Base Mitigation: Implement hierarchical routing structure, use route configuration modules, implement route guards systematically, document routing architecture, and use route naming conventions.

Risk Title: Test Suite Scalability and Execution Time
Risk Description: As the application grows, test suites become slow to execute, difficult to maintain, and may not provide adequate coverage, impacting CI/CD pipeline efficiency.
Base Mitigation: Implement test parallelization, use shallow rendering for unit tests, optimize test setup and teardown, implement test categorization (unit, integration, e2e), and use test coverage tools.

Risk Title: Code Quality Degradation in Large Teams
Risk Description: Multiple teams working on the same Angular codebase without proper standards and governance leads to inconsistent code quality, architectural drift, and integration conflicts.
Base Mitigation: Establish coding standards and style guides, implement automated linting and formatting (ESLint, Prettier), use code review processes, implement architectural decision records (ADRs), and provide team training.

Risk Description: Managing translations and locale-specific content for multiple languages becomes complex and error-prone as the application scales, impacting maintenance and deployment.
Base Mitigation: Use Angular's built-in i18n support or libraries like ngx-translate, implement translation management workflows, use translation keys consistently, and automate translation file generation.

Risk Description: Unhandled exceptions in Angular components, services, or observables can crash the application or leave it in an inconsistent state, degrading user experience and data integrity.
Base Mitigation: Implement global error handler using ErrorHandler, use RxJS error handling operators (catchError, retry), implement proper try-catch blocks, and integrate error monitoring tools (Sentry, Rollbar).

Risk Description: Poor handling of HTTP errors, network failures, or timeout scenarios leads to poor user experience, data inconsistencies, and application instability.
Base Mitigation: Implement HTTP interceptors for centralized error handling, use retry strategies with exponential backoff, provide user-friendly error messages, implement offline detection, and log errors for monitoring.

Risk Description: Angular applications may exhibit different behaviors or failures across browsers due to varying JavaScript engine implementations, CSS support, or API availability.
Base Mitigation: Test on all target browsers, use Angular's browser compatibility configuration, include appropriate polyfills, implement feature detection, and use Browserslist configuration.

Risk Title: Mobile Device and Touch Event Handling Issues
Risk Description: Improper handling of touch events, viewport configurations, or mobile-specific behaviors can cause functionality failures or poor user experience on mobile devices.
Base Mitigation: Implement responsive design with proper viewport meta tags, test on actual mobile devices, use touch-friendly UI components, implement proper gesture handling, and use mobile-first development approach.

Risk Description: Improper use of Angular lifecycle hooks (ngOnInit, ngOnDestroy, etc.) can lead to memory leaks, race conditions, or unexpected behavior during component initialization and destruction.
Base Mitigation: Follow Angular lifecycle hook best practices, clean up resources in ngOnDestroy, avoid heavy operations in constructors, use appropriate hooks for specific tasks, and document lifecycle dependencies.

Risk Description: Concurrent asynchronous operations without proper synchronization can lead to race conditions, data inconsistencies, and unpredictable application behavior.
Base Mitigation: Use RxJS operators for proper sequencing (switchMap, concatMap, exhaustMap), implement proper state management, use async/await with proper error handling, and test concurrent scenarios.

Risk Description: Dependencies on third-party libraries expose the application to breaking changes, deprecations, or library abandonment, potentially causing application failures after updates.
Base Mitigation: Pin dependency versions, review changelogs before updates, implement comprehensive testing before upgrading, maintain fallback strategies, and evaluate library maintenance status before adoption.

Risk Title: Poor Offline and Network Failure Handling
Risk Description: Applications without proper offline detection and handling provide poor user experience during network failures and may lose user data or enter inconsistent states.
Base Mitigation: Implement service workers for offline support, use Angular PWA capabilities, implement network status detection, provide offline indicators, and implement data synchronization strategies.

Risk Title: Form Data Loss on Navigation or Errors
Risk Description: User-entered form data can be lost during navigation, browser refresh, or application errors, leading to poor user experience and potential data loss.
Base Mitigation: Implement form state persistence using localStorage or sessionStorage, use CanDeactivate guards to warn users, implement auto-save functionality, and provide draft recovery mechanisms.

Risk Description: Inconsistencies between client-side and server-side data due to failed updates, concurrent modifications, or network issues can lead to data corruption and user confusion.
Base Mitigation: Implement optimistic updates with rollback mechanisms, use version control or ETags for conflict detection, implement proper error handling and retry logic, and provide conflict resolution UI.

Risk Description: Direct mutation of state objects in Angular applications can lead to change detection issues, unpredictable behavior, and data inconsistencies, especially when using OnPush change detection.
Base Mitigation: Enforce immutability patterns, use immutable data structures or libraries (Immer), implement state management with immutability enforcement (NgRx), and use TypeScript readonly modifiers.

Risk Description: Lack of comprehensive validation or inconsistencies between client and server validation can allow invalid data to be processed, leading to data corruption or application errors.
Base Mitigation: Implement consistent validation rules on client and server, use Angular Reactive Forms with validators, create reusable validation functions, and validate data at component boundaries.

Risk Description: Cached data in Angular services or browser storage can become stale, leading to users seeing outdated information and making decisions based on incorrect data.
Base Mitigation: Implement cache invalidation strategies, use cache expiration timestamps, provide manual refresh mechanisms, implement cache versioning, and use appropriate cache-control headers.

Risk Description: Errors in data transformation logic between API responses and application models can lead to data loss, type mismatches, or incorrect data representation.
Base Mitigation: Use TypeScript interfaces and type guards, implement data transformation services with unit tests, validate transformed data, use libraries like class-transformer, and document data models.

Risk Title: Lost Updates Due to Concurrent Modifications
Risk Description: Multiple concurrent operations modifying the same data without proper coordination can result in lost updates where one operation overwrites changes from another.
Base Mitigation: Implement optimistic locking with version numbers, use last-write-wins with timestamps, provide conflict detection and resolution UI, and implement proper transaction handling.

Risk Description: Deploying Angular applications without proper production optimizations (AOT compilation, minification, tree-shaking) results in larger bundle sizes, slower performance, and potential runtime errors.
Base Mitigation: Always use Angular CLI production build (ng build --prod), configure angular.json for production optimizations, implement build validation in CI/CD pipeline, and test production builds before deployment.

Risk Description: Incorrect environment configurations or using development settings in production can expose sensitive information, cause functionality failures, or degrade performance.
Base Mitigation: Use Angular environment files properly, implement environment-specific build configurations, validate environment settings in deployment pipeline, and use configuration management tools.

Risk Description: Insufficient automated testing in CI/CD pipelines allows bugs and regressions to reach production, increasing the risk of deployment failures and production incidents.
Base Mitigation: Implement comprehensive test suites (unit, integration, e2e) in CI/CD, use code coverage thresholds, implement automated security scanning, and use staging environments for pre-production validation.

Risk Description: Without proper rollback mechanisms, failed deployments can result in extended downtime and inability to quickly restore service when issues are discovered in production.
Base Mitigation: Implement blue-green or canary deployment strategies, maintain previous version artifacts, automate rollback procedures, implement health checks and monitoring, and document rollback procedures.

Risk Description: Improper cache invalidation strategies for Angular assets on CDNs can cause users to receive outdated application versions, leading to functionality issues and poor user experience.
Base Mitigation: Implement file hashing in build process, configure proper cache-control headers, use CDN cache invalidation APIs, implement versioning strategies, and test cache behavior after deployments.

Risk Description: Improperly configured Docker containers for Angular applications can lead to security vulnerabilities, performance issues, or deployment failures in containerized environments.
Base Mitigation: Use multi-stage Docker builds, minimize container image size, use non-root users, implement proper health checks, scan images for vulnerabilities, and follow Docker best practices.

Risk Description: Lack of proper monitoring and observability in production makes it difficult to detect issues, diagnose problems, and understand application behavior and performance.
Base Mitigation: Implement application performance monitoring (APM), use error tracking services (Sentry, Rollbar), implement custom metrics and logging, set up alerting for critical issues, and use Real User Monitoring (RUM).

Risk Description: Manual deployment processes are error-prone and can lead to inconsistent deployments, configuration mistakes, and increased deployment time and risk.
Base Mitigation: Fully automate deployment processes using CI/CD tools, implement infrastructure as code, use deployment checklists, implement automated smoke tests post-deployment, and document deployment procedures.

Risk Title: Poor Version Control and Release Management
Risk Description: Inadequate version control practices and release management can lead to deployment of wrong versions, difficulty in tracking changes, and challenges in coordinating releases.
Base Mitigation: Implement semantic versioning, use Git branching strategies (GitFlow, trunk-based), tag releases properly, maintain changelog, implement release notes automation, and use release management tools.

Risk Description: Inconsistent dependency versions between development and production environments can cause unexpected behavior or failures when deploying Angular applications.
Base Mitigation: Use package-lock.json or yarn.lock consistently, implement dependency version validation in CI/CD, use containerization for consistent environments, and document dependency requirements.

Risk Description: Deploying Angular Universal applications with server-side rendering introduces additional complexity, potential server-side vulnerabilities, and operational challenges.
Base Mitigation: Use proper Node.js server configuration, implement server monitoring and scaling, secure server-side code, implement proper error handling for SSR failures, and use managed SSR platforms when possible.

Risk Title: Service Worker Update and Caching Issues
Risk Description: Improper service worker configuration or update strategies in Angular PWAs can cause users to be stuck on old versions or experience caching issues after deployments.
Base Mitigation: Implement proper service worker update strategies, use Angular PWA module correctly, test service worker behavior, implement version checking and update prompts, and provide manual refresh options.
